Multi-Omics Analysis Report: Type 2 Diabetes in Skeletal Muscle

Integrated RNA-seq, RRBS methylation, and ChIP-seq analysis of T2D vs. healthy controls in human M. vastus lateralis

1. RNA-seq: Quality Control and Sample Concordance

1.1 Coverage distribution

Figure 1. Distribution of overall mean gene coverage across all 6 samples. The bimodal shape is typical of RNA-seq: the left peak represents genes with zero or near-zero expression (not transcribed in skeletal muscle), and the right peak represents robustly detected genes. Genes with mean coverage < 10 were filtered out for downstream analysis, removing ~49,500 low-signal genes and retaining ~13,600 expressed genes.

Figure 2. Per-sample coverage distributions for T2D (SRR17948859, SRR17948866) and Active control (SRR17948869, SRR17948871, SRR17948873, SRR17948874) samples. The distributions are highly consistent across samples, indicating uniform sequencing depth and no sample-level technical outliers.

1.2 Sample correlation

Figure 3. Pearson correlation heatmap of log-transformed gene counts (all genes). The heatmap is symmetric, with lighter colors indicating higher correlation. Samples within the same group (Active: 8869, 8873, 8871; T2D: 8859, 8866, 8874) tend to correlate more strongly, although the separation is not perfectly clean due to noise from unexpressed genes.

Figure 4. Pearson correlation heatmap after filtering (mean coverage >= 10). Removing noisy low-count genes improves inter-sample correlation and sharpens the group structure. The dendrogram now separates the three Active controls from the three T2D samples.

1.3 PCA

Figure 5. Principal Component Analysis on CPM-normalized, filtered counts. PC1 (24.9% variance) separates T2D samples (cyan, left) from Active controls (red, right), confirming that the largest source of transcriptional variation in this dataset is the disease state. PC2 captures within-group biological variability.

1.4 Differential expression (DESeq2)

DESeq2 was run with design formula ~ group (T2D vs. Active). Genes were called significant at |log2FC| > 1.3 and padj < 0.05.

Direction Count
Upregulated in T2D 143
Downregulated in T2D 90
Total DEGs 233

The asymmetry (more up- than downregulated genes) is consistent with activation of stress-response, inflammatory, and metabolic remodeling pathways in insulin-resistant skeletal muscle.

2. RRBS: DNA Methylation Profiling

2.1 Preprocessing summary

Metric Value
Trimming Trim Galore --rrbs --clip_R1 3 --clip_R2 3
Alignment rate (Bismark) 63–66%
Bisulfite conversion efficiency ~93%
CpG context methylation ~50% of CpGs
Non-CpG methylation < 1%
Genome-wide average methylation ~7.7%

The < 1% non-CpG methylation confirms high bisulfite conversion efficiency (expected in somatic tissue). The ~7.7% genome-wide average reflects the RRBS enrichment bias toward CpG-dense (and therefore often unmethylated) regions.

2.2 Methylation distributions

Figure 6. CpG methylation percentage distributions for all four samples. The characteristic bimodal pattern — dominant peaks at 0% and 100% — reflects the binary nature of mammalian CpG methylation: most CpGs are either fully unmethylated (active promoters, CpG islands) or fully methylated (gene bodies, repetitive elements). The distributions are remarkably consistent between T2D and control samples, indicating that global methylation architecture is preserved.

2.3 Coverage

Figure 7. CpG coverage distribution. Most CpGs have 10–100x coverage (log10 = 1–2), which is adequate for reliable methylation calling. The filtering step (lo.count = 10, hi.perc = 99.9) removed < 0.1% of CpGs.

2.4 Sample concordance

Figure 8. Spearman correlation matrix of CpG methylation levels. All pairwise correlations are 0.91–0.92, indicating highly concordant methylation profiles — expected for the same tissue type (M. vastus lateralis). The scatter plots in the lower triangle confirm the strong linear relationship between samples.

Figure 9. Hierarchical clustering (Ward’s method, Euclidean distance). Interestingly, T2D_2 and Ctrl_1 cluster together, and T2D_1 with Ctrl_2 — an unexpected pattern that may reflect batch effects or biological heterogeneity within the small sample size (n=2 per group).

Figure 10. PCA of CpG methylation data. T2D samples (blue) and controls (red) separate along PC1, confirming that disease-associated methylation differences exist despite the high overall concordance and the mixed clustering pattern.

2.5 Differential methylation

Differentially methylated positions (DMPs) were identified with methylKit using: - Delta-beta >= 10% (minimum methylation difference) - Hochberg q-value < 0.05 (multiple testing correction)

Figure 11. Distribution of hyper- and hypomethylated CpGs across chromosomes. Standard chromosomes (1–22, X) show 0.5–1% hyper/hypomethylated CpGs. Random and alternative assembly contigs sometimes show extreme percentages due to their small size.

2.6 Genomic annotation (genomation)

Figure 12. Genomic annotation of DMPs with genomation. 81% of DMPs fall outside CpG islands (“Other”), 11% in CpG shores (2 kb flanking regions), and only 9% directly within CpG islands. This is consistent with the current understanding that disease-associated methylation changes preferentially affect CpG shores and open-sea regions, a pattern originally described in cancer and also observed in metabolic disorders.

3. ChIP-seq: RING1 (Polycomb) Binding

3.1 IGV visualization (chr10)

Figure 13. IGV session showing RING1 ChIP-seq signal on chromosome 10. Yellow tracks: input controls. Blue tracks: RING1 ChIP experiments. Clear enrichment peaks are visible in experimental samples (blue) but absent in matched inputs (yellow), confirming specific RING1 binding. Both narrow peaks (sharp, TF-like binding) and broad peaks (extended Polycomb domains) are observed.

3.2 Peak overlap analysis

Figure 14. Venn diagrams comparing RING1 peaks between breast fibroblast (BF) and human skin fibroblast (HS) tissues. Left (bedtools intersect): 1,355 common peaks using coordinate overlap. Right (macs2 bdgdiff): 689 common peaks using a statistical likelihood-ratio test. bdgdiff is more conservative because it accounts for local background variation, whereas bedtools uses simple genomic overlap.

The ~1,300 shared peaks represent the core RING1 binding programme conserved across fibroblast lineages. Tissue-specific peaks (BF-unique: 2,286; HS-unique: 3,050) may reflect lineage-specific Polycomb targets involved in cell identity maintenance.

4. Multi-Omics Integration

4.1 Strategy

RNA-seq DEGs (233 genes, padj < 0.05, |log2FC| > 1.3) were overlapped with RRBS DMPs (delta-beta >= 10%, q < 0.05) by matching DMP genomic coordinates to gene bodies. For each gene containing one or more DMPs, methylation changes were aggregated using Stouffer’s method for p-value combination.

4.2 Interpretation framework

Expression Methylation Biological interpretation
Upregulated Hypomethylated Promoter demethylation removes silencing → gene activation
Downregulated Hypermethylated Promoter gains methylation → gene silencing
Upregulated Hypermethylated Discordant — may reflect gene body methylation (associated with active transcription)
Downregulated Hypomethylated Discordant — may reflect enhancer or distal regulatory element effects

Genes in the concordant quadrants (up + hypo, down + hyper) provide the strongest evidence for epigenetically driven transcriptional changes in T2D skeletal muscle and are priority candidates for functional validation.

5. Conclusions

  1. RNA-seq identified 233 differentially expressed genes (143 up, 90 down) in T2D muscle with clear PCA separation, indicating a substantial transcriptional shift.

  2. RRBS revealed differentially methylated CpG positions between T2D and controls, predominantly in non-CpG-island regions (shores and open sea), consistent with disease-associated methylation patterns.

  3. ChIP-seq mapped RING1 (PRC1) binding across two fibroblast lineages, identifying ~1,300 shared peaks and tissue-specific Polycomb targets with both narrow (TF-like) and broad (Polycomb domain) peak architecture.

  4. Integration of DEG and DMP data identifies genes with concordant transcriptional and epigenetic changes, providing multi-layered evidence for epigenetic dysregulation in T2D pathophysiology.

Methods

Step Tool Key parameters
RNA-seq alignment STAR --quantMode GeneCounts, hg38, ~95% mapped
Differential expression DESeq2 |log2FC| > 1.3, padj < 0.05
RRBS trimming Trim Galore --rrbs --clip_R1 3 --clip_R2 3
Bisulfite alignment Bismark Bowtie2 backend, 63–66% mapped
Differential methylation methylKit Δβ >= 10%, Hochberg q < 0.05
Genomic annotation genomation CpG islands (hg38)
GO enrichment rGREAT basalPlusExt rule
ChIP-seq alignment Bowtie2 --very-sensitive, hg38
Peak calling MACS2 -f BAM -g hs, d = 118 bp
Peak comparison bedtools / macs2 bdgdiff intersect, subtract
Peak annotation HOMER annotatePeaks.pl, findMotifs.pl
